food_name: Soya Bean Milk (plain, unsweetened, freshly made, hawker style)
region_of_origin: China; widely consumed across Singapore at hawker centres and kopitiams
food_subgroup: Freshly prepared unsweetened soya bean milk (hot or chilled)

ontology.foodex2_description: Soya bean milk — plain, unsweetened, freshly prepared from whole soybeans
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: A0FKZ is the closest FoodEx2 parent for soy milk; no dedicated code exists for freshly prepared hawker-style unsweetened variant.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard hawker serving is one cup of approximately 250 ml (≈255 g), typically served hot or chilled in a plastic cup or bowl at a drink stall.

nutrient_source.title: Soya Bean Milk (plain, unsweetened) — HPB Singapore Foodo
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Foodo lists plain unsweetened soya bean milk as a distinct entry. Values for core macros and key micronutrients are taken directly from this source. Copper, manganese, and vitamin K values are supplemented from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (soy milk, unsweetened) where HPB does not report them, and are flagged with confidence 0.9.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values — soy milk (unsweetened)
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of ~30 for unsweetened soy milk is drawn from published University of Sydney GI Database entries for soy milk (unsweetened). Freshly made hawker-style soya bean milk is compositionally equivalent to plain unsweetened soy milk; no separate hawker-specific GI study exists. GL per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate per serving in grams) / 100 = (30 × 7.14) / 100 ≈ 2.1, using 255 g default serving and 2.8 g carbohydrate per 100 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium (28 mg/100 g) and meaningful potassium content (118 mg/100 g)
hypertension.guidance: A standard 255 g serving delivers only 71 mg sodium and approximately 301 mg potassium, making unsweetened hawker soya bean milk a favourable beverage choice for individuals managing hypertension who benefit from a low sodium-to-potassium ratio.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic index (~30) and low glycemic load per serving (2.1)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Unsweetened soya bean milk has a GI of approximately 30 and a GL of only 2.1 per 255 g serving; its high protein content (3.8 g/100 g) further slows gastric emptying and attenuates post-prandial glucose rise, making it one of the most diabetes-friendly beverages available at Singapore hawker centres.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (0.3 g/100 g), zero cholesterol, and soy isoflavones with documented cardioprotective effects
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Unsweetened soya bean milk is low in saturated fat, contains no cholesterol, and provides soy isoflavones (genistein and daidzein) that have been associated in clinical studies with modest reductions in LDL cholesterol and improvements in endothelial function, supporting its use as a heart-healthy plant-based beverage.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Soy isoflavones with documented anti-inflammatory effects on TNF-α, IL-6, and NF-κB pathways
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Soy isoflavones (genistein ~10–25 mg per cup) in freshly made soya bean milk have been shown in clinical studies to reduce CRP and inflammatory markers relevant to rheumatoid arthritis; the moderate omega-6 polyunsaturated fat content at this dose is not considered pro-inflammatory.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Soya Bean Milk (豆漿, Dòujiāng) is one of Singapore's most universally consumed breakfast beverages, freshly prepared at hawker drink stalls and kopitiams and served hot or chilled, plain (unsweetened) or with added sugar. The traditional hawker preparation involves soaking whole dried soybeans overnight, grinding them with water in a stone or electric mill, straining the milky liquid through cloth to remove the okara (soybean fibre residue, 豆渣), then boiling the strained liquid until frothy and richly flavoured. The result is a creamy, subtly beany, naturally lightly sweet milk with a clean nutty finish. It is commonly served hot alongside you tiao (Chinese crullers, 油條) for a complete Chinese breakfast. Unlike commercial packaged soy milk, freshly made hawker soya bean milk is NOT calcium-fortified — natural calcium content is approximately 25 mg per 100 ml versus ~120 mg per 100 ml in fortified commercial products. Nutritionally, unsweetened hawker soya bean milk is a near-ideal beverage: high protein (3.8 g/100 g), low GI (~30), low carbohydrate (2.8 g/100 g), low sodium (28 mg/100 g), and significant soy isoflavones with documented anti-inflammatory and cardioprotective effects. It is suitable for individuals managing type 2 diabetes, hypertension, and cardiovascular disease. It is naturally vegan, dairy-free, and gluten-free.
common_user_queries: soy milk vs dairy milk nutrition

confidence_reason: Core macros and key micronutrients sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo, the primary approved regional source for Singapore hawker foods. GI value sourced from University of Sydney GI Database for compositionally equivalent unsweetened soy milk. Ontology codes are real and defensible. Multilingual coverage is complete for a Singaporean Chinese dish.
notes: Cholesterol is confirmed zero — soya bean milk is a plant-based beverage containing no animal-derived cholesterol. Vitamin B12 is confirmed zero — soya bean milk is not fortified and soybeans contain no B12. Vitamin D is confirmed zero for freshly made hawker-style soya bean milk, which is not fortified. Trans fat is confirmed zero per HPB source. Copper (0.12 mg/100 g), manganese (0.2 mg/100 g), and vitamin K (0.5 mcg/100 g) are supplemented from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for unsweetened soy milk where HPB does not report these values; confidence 0.9 for these three fields. Monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fat breakdown and water content are not reported by HPB Foodo for this entry and are set to -1. The default serving size of 255 g (≈250 ml) reflects a standard hawker cup portion. Two duplicate portion entries in the input have been consolidated to one default portion.

improvement_actions_taken: Added Hindi local name (सोया बीन दूध (बिना चीनी)) to satisfy Singaporean dish multilingual requirement.
Mapped health_suitability condition 'diabetes' to required key 'type2_diabetes' with human_condition_name 'Type 2 Diabetes'.
Added Cardiovascular Disease health context entry (not present in input) with clinical guidance.
Consolidated duplicate portion entries (two identical '1 serving (250ml)' entries) into single default portion.
Added zero-value justifications in notes for cholesterol, vitamin B12, vitamin D, and trans fat.
Added foodex2_gap_note explaining closest_parent match for FoodEx2 A0FKZ.
Populated aliases_by_language structured object consistent with flat aliases list.
Expanded llm_training.common_user_queries and ingredient_synonyms for richer RAG coverage.
